description: Legislation protecting consumer rights against unfair trade practices, defective goods, and deficient services

sections.title: Definition of Consumer
sections.text: Consumer means any person who buys any goods for a consideration which has been paid or promised or partly paid and partly promised, or under any system of deferred payment, and includes any user of such goods other than the person who buys such goods; or hires or avails of any service for a consideration.
sections.simplified: A consumer is anyone who buys goods or services for personal use. Both buyers and users are considered consumers. However, if you buy goods for resale or commercial purposes, you are NOT a consumer.

sections.title: Definition of Complaint
sections.text: Complaint means any allegation in writing made by a complainant that an unfair trade practice or a restrictive trade practice has been adopted by any trader or service provider; the goods bought by him or agreed to be bought by him suffer from one or more defects; the services hired or availed of or agreed to be hired or availed of by him suffer from any deficiency in any respect.
sections.simplified: A consumer complaint can be about: defective goods, poor services, overcharging, misleading ads, or unfair business practices.

sections.title: Definition of Unfair Trade Practice
sections.text: Unfair trade practice means a trade practice which, for the purpose of promoting the sale, use or supply of any goods or for the provision of any service, adopts any unfair method or unfair or deceptive practice including false representation, misleading advertisement, permitting publication of any advertisement for sale or supply of goods or services at a bargain price.
sections.simplified: Unfair trade practices include: false or misleading ads, fake discounts, hidden charges, misrepresentation of product quality, fake reviews, and deceptive marketing. All of these are actionable under consumer law.

sections.title: Establishment of Consumer Disputes Redressal Commissions
sections.text: There shall be established Consumer Disputes Redressal Commissions known as District Commission, State Commission and National Commission.
sections.simplified: Consumer complaints are handled at 3 levels: 1) District Commission (up to ₹1 crore), 2) State Commission (₹1 crore to ₹10 crore), 3) National Commission (above ₹10 crore). You can file complaints online too.

sections.title: Jurisdiction of District Commission
sections.text: Subject to the provisions of this Act, the District Commission shall have jurisdiction to entertain complaints where the value of the goods or services paid as consideration does not exceed one crore rupees.
sections.simplified: District Consumer Commission handles complaints up to ₹1 crore. This is the most accessible level for most consumer disputes.

sections.title: Manner in which complaint shall be made
sections.text: A complaint in relation to any goods sold or delivered or agreed to be sold, or any service provided or agreed to be provided, may be filed with the District Commission by the consumer to whom such goods are sold or delivered or such service is provided.
sections.simplified: You can file a consumer complaint: 1) In person at the District Commission, 2) Online at consumerhelpline.gov.in or edaakhil.nic.in, 3) Through a lawyer (optional — you can represent yourself), 4) No court fee is required for filing.

sections.title: Penalties for misleading advertisement
sections.text: The Central Authority may, sue motu or on a complaint received from any person, inquire or cause an inquiry into cases of violation of consumer rights, unfair trade practices and false or misleading advertisements.
sections.simplified: The Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA) can take action against misleading ads. Penalties: up to ₹10 lakh fine and 2 years imprisonment for first offence. Up to ₹50 lakh and 5 years for repeat offences.

sections.title: Complaint can be filed electronically
sections.text: A complaint may be filed from the place of residence or business of the complainant. The complaint may be filed electronically.
sections.simplified: You can file consumer complaints ONLINE from anywhere in India. You don't need to travel to the location where the goods were purchased. Use edaakhil.nic.in for online filing.

sections.title: Relief available to consumers
sections.text: The District Commission may remove the defect or deficiency in the goods or services; replace the goods with new goods of similar description; return to the complainant the price paid; pay compensation for any loss or injury suffered; remove defects or deficiencies in the goods or services; discontinue the unfair trade practice or not to repeat them; pay adequate costs to parties.
sections.simplified: If your complaint is successful, you can get: replacement or repair of defective goods, full refund, compensation for loss and suffering, and the seller/company may be ordered to stop unfair practices.

sections.title: Appeal from District Commission
sections.text: Any person aggrieved by an order made by the District Commission may prefer an appeal against such order to the State Commission within a period of forty-five days from the date of the order.
sections.simplified: If you lose at the District Commission, you can appeal to the State Commission within 45 days. Further appeal can be made to the National Commission.

sections.title: Liability of product manufacturer
sections.text: A product manufacturer shall be liable in a product liability action if the product contains a manufacturing defect, is defective in design, if there is a deviation from manufacturing specifications, the product does not conform to the express warranty, or the product does not contain adequate instructions of correct usage.
sections.simplified: Product manufacturers are liable if: the product has manufacturing defects, design flaws, doesn't match specifications, doesn't meet warranty claims, or lacks proper usage instructions. You can sue the manufacturer directly.

sections.title: Liability of product service provider
sections.text: A product service provider shall be liable in a product liability action, if the service provided by him was faulty or imperfect or deficient or inadequate in quality, nature or manner of performance.
sections.simplified: Service providers (hospitals, banks, telecom, etc.) are liable if their service is deficient, negligent, or below standard. This includes medical negligence, banking errors, and poor telecom service.

sections.title: Liability of product seller
sections.text: A product seller who is not a product manufacturer shall be liable in a product liability action if he has exercised substantial control over the designing, testing, manufacturing, packaging or labelling of a product that caused harm; or he has altered or modified the product and such alteration or modification was the substantial factor in causing the harm.
sections.simplified: Sellers (shops, online platforms) can also be held liable if they: modified the product, made false claims, sold expired goods, or didn't ensure the product met safety standards.

sections.title: Power to issue safety notices, recall products
sections.text: The Central Authority may issue safety notice relating to any goods which are dangerous, unsafe, or are likely to be dangerous or unsafe.
sections.simplified: The government can issue product recalls, safety warnings, and ban dangerous products. Companies that continue to sell recalled or banned products face severe penalties.

sections.title: Ecommerce rules and consumer rights online
sections.text: The Central Government may make rules for preventing unfair trade practices in e-commerce, direct selling, and for protecting the interests of consumers.
sections.simplified: E-commerce platforms (Amazon, Flipkart, etc.) must: display seller details, provide easy returns/refunds, not manipulate prices, show genuine reviews, and have a grievance mechanism. You have full consumer rights for online purchases.
